title: Hybrid quant & batching microbench: TRT-LLM vs vLLM profiling
preview:
Header:
Date: 2027-11-02
Start: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 57 minutes
Location: Zoom
Attendees: Ben Carter (Redwood AE), Priya Narayanan (Redwood SE), Liam O'Neill (Redwood Principal SE), Aisha Khan (CTO, Nimbus), Evan Li (SRE Lead, Nimbus), Marcos Ruiz (ML Infra, Nimbus)
Shared artifacts: short deck link (internal): https://rwd.company/share/nimbus-microbench.pdf, bench sheet: /redwood/benchmarks/nimbus-pilot.xlsx

Summary:
Quick technical deep dive to align on microbench scope: compare TensorRT-LLM (TRT-LLM) cudagraph-enabled runs vs vLLM optimized runtime under hybrid quant profiles, verify KV cache behavior across sequential batches, and validate VPC/private-cluster connectivity for a 3-day pilot. Customer cares about 95th latency, tail stability under burst traffic, and on-prem constraints (HSM/KMS).

Topics:
- Test matrix: 7B/13B/50B, seq len 512/2048, batch sizes 1/4/8
- Quant profiles: FP16, int8 hybrid (activation dynamic), per-channel symmetric vs asymmetric
- CUDA graph replay behavior, warm vs cold KV cache hits
- Batching strategies: pipelined batching, sticky KV segments, prefetch heuristics
- Private deployment constraints: VPC peering, egress, KMS/HSM for keys

Transcript Body:
[00:00] Ben: all right folks, thanks for joining, uh this is Ben from Redwood. today we've got Priya and Liam on the call to walk through the microbench plan and then dig into a few live traces. Aisha, Evan, Marcos, thanks for making time. do you want to kick off with what success looks like from Nimbus' side?

[00:22] Aisha: yeah thank you. main things are throughput predictability for our trading analytics, 95th latency under 120ms for inference streams, and we need to keep data in our VPC. cost is secondary but still important; if TPU style instances or NVLink multi-gpu are needed we can talk budget.

[00:45] Priya: great. quick clarif, when you say 95th at 120ms, is that for 512 token windows or 2k sequences? those are very different numbers.

[00:59] Aisha: good point. target is for 512 token average live queries — the 2k windows are for longer context replays but lower QPS.

[01:15] Liam: ok, that helps. we'll map the microbench to two lanes: low-latency lane (512 seq, batch 1-4) and high-throughput lane (2k seq, batch up to 8), and compare TRT-LLM cudagraphs vs vLLM's async scheduler.

[02:10] Evan: one ask — can we exercise KV eviction? our service sometimes hits KV thrash during bursts, causing latency spikes. we want to see both median and tail effects.

[02:25] Priya: yes, we'll include eviction-aware traces. plan to run synthetic prefix reuse patterns: hot prefixes (80% reuse), warm (50%), cold (0%) to show KV hit ratios. we'll capture KV hit/miss counters and latency histograms.

[03:05] Marcos: also, our security team insists on KMS for model artifacts and HSM for signing tokens, so private deployment path must show KMS integration points.

[03:20] Ben: noted. we'll include the security docs in the follow-up.

[03:45] Liam: jumping into profiling notes — we ran an internal microbench last week. quick call-out: TRT-LLM with cudagraph warm paths gave lower median for 50B at batch 4 but its 95th increased when the cudagraph had to be replayed because of dynamic sequence divergence. vLLM's async path had slightly higher median but more stable 95th in our runs.

[04:20] Aisha: can you quantify? numbers help.

[04:30] Priya: reference numbers from our internal run (50B, seq 512, batch 4): vLLM median ~11.8 ms per token, 95th ~28 ms; TRT-LLM median ~9.9 ms, 95th ~42 ms when graph replays happened. but in steady-state cudagraphs reduce overhead. importantly hybrid int8 profiles reduced memory and increased throughput but introduced some quantization noise on certain tokens — we have mitigations.

[05:15] Evan: hmm that 95th jump is concerning. is the replay pattern deterministic? can we force cudagraph stability?

[05:30] Liam: good question. cudagraph replay occurs when input shapes or execution paths change — e.g., kv length changes or different attention patterns for long-tail prompts. mitigation approaches: conservative graph grouping (split by seq bucket), fall back to vLLM-like dynamic path for outliers, or use sticky KV segments so longer prompts don't cause partial replay. each has tradeoffs — more graphs equals more memory.

[06:10] Aisha: memory tradeoffs are ok if we can keep 95th under 120ms. what's the multi-gpu story? we run NVLink-enabled A40s in our colo.

[06:25] Priya: NVLink helps with KV cache sharding; TRT-LLM can pipeline across GPUs but cudagraphs become more fragile across multi-gpu boundaries. vLLM's sharded KV with async prefetch tends to be more resilient in multi-gpu setups. we should test both on your exact hardware.

[07:00] Marcos: we'll provision a 2-node NVLink setup for the pilot. also want to try quant profiles: full FP16, int8 per-channel, and a hybrid where attention matrices are int8 and residuals stay FP16.

[07:20] Ben: that hybrid approach is interesting — tends to keep quality while saving memory. Priya, are there known degens in that profile?

[07:30] Priya: some models show token-level degeneration in repetition-heavy prompts; we'll run evals with your prompt set and auto-run quality regression checks. we'll include perplexity and targeted prompt tests from the deck.

[08:10] Evan: what telemetry will you need from our side? NVTX traces? sysstat?

[08:20] Liam: NVTX + cudagraph logs + perf counters (sm, l2) + vLLM event logs are ideal. we'll provide a small collector script. we'll also ask for synthetic traffic generator config so we can reproduce dates.

[09:00] Aisha: timeline — can you start the pilot next week? we want results before end of month to decide Q1 procurement.

[09:12] Ben: yes. proposed: day 0-1 provisioning, day 2-4 microbench runs across matrix, day 5-7 deeper fuzzing for cudagraph edge cases. total 7 days.

[09:35] Marcos: ok we'll aim to have the VPC/test keys ready by Friday.

[09:50] Priya: a few gotchas we want to call out: 1) batch coalescing — if you have bursty tiny requests, continuous batching helps but increases tail. 2) KV prefetcher — prefetch depth trades off memory vs prefetch hit rate. 3) quant calibration datasets — important to match your prompt distribution or int8 will regress.

[10:40] Evan: about prefetch depth — do you have heuristics? we run some steady streams and big bursts from our front end.

[10:55] Priya: heuristics: set base prefetch to avg_kv_span*1.5 and apply hysteresis on eviction events. for bursty workloads enable adaptive prefetch that ramps up on QPS signals. we'll include param suggestions in the runbook.

[12:00] Aisha: any blockers on compliance? SOC2?

[12:10] Ben: no blockers; we'll attach our SOC2 summary and SSO/SAML flow. for HSM we'll need a short onboarding doc; Priya will loop security docs.

[12:40] Liam: small technical aside — when measuring latency we prefer token-level histograms rather than whole request because the model can emit long sequences and start-token latency is often most relevant. also include GPU occupancy and CUDA stream wait times.

[13:20] Marcos: can you dump an example trace now? even a snippet so our infra team sees format.

[13:35] Priya: sure, I'll share the NVTX snippet in chat, but quick excerpt: [NVTX] cudagraph_replay start 00:00:12.342, graph_exec 00:00:12.363, kv_cache_miss bucket 4, latency spike 31ms. we'll put full traces into the sheet.

[14:05] Evan: any thought on fallback policies? auto-fallback to smaller model or slower vLLM path?

[14:20] Liam: yes we recommend two-tier fallback: first try smaller compatible variant (e.g., quantized 13B), then fallback to a dynamic runtime path that avoids cudagraphs. Redwood's smart routing can keep a per-customer policy.

[14:50] Aisha: ok sounds good. can you send a sample policy we can review?

[15:00] Ben: will do. Priya will attach sample routing policy and cost estimates.

[15:25] Priya: final note on quality: we'll run an automated delta test on your prompt set and flag any tokens exceeding configured tolerance. if needed we can recommend different quant scales or per-channel calibrations.

[16:00] Aisha: perfect. next steps?

[16:10] Ben: recap of next steps: 1) Priya share microbench spreadsheet + collector script (due 11/04), 2) Marcos provision VPC/test cluster and keys (due 11/05), 3) Ben schedule pilot kickoff (due 11/08). we'll also share security summary and SOC2 artifacts.

[16:40] Evan: thanks all — appreciate the level of detail. looking forward to the pilot.

[16:55] Ben: thanks everyone. we'll follow up in email with links and owners. bye.

title: Long context + high TPS stress walkthrough - Redwood demo
preview: Redwood AE/SE walk through an edge-case focused demo simulating long conversation contexts, high TPS, and streaming backpressure. Customer is evaluating hosted vs dedicated for a real-time analytics assistant. Key asks: tail-latency under load, graceful streaming reconnects, quantization/kv cache effects on long context, and SLA pricing for bursty traffic.

title: Moonlit Devs — Onramp timing and privacy considerations
preview: Discovery call focused on Moonlit Devs' need to get to first API call quickly while evaluating privacy (VPC/on-prem) requirements and whether to start hosted with free credits or pursue a Dedicated/private pilot. Key concerns: data residency, KMS, audit logs, latency SLOs for inference, and cost tradeoffs between hosted vs. dedicated. Agreed next steps include security docs, free credits, and a scoped pilot to run benchmarks.

title: MercuryBank QBR / reliability review
preview:
Meeting: MercuryBank QBR / reliability review
Recorded: 2026-03-10 16:00 UTC
Duration: 42 minutes
Attendees: Ben Carter, Ava Chen, Dev Patel, Jordan Wells, Sana Iqbal

Summary:
- Overall satisfaction good; throughput is meeting targets.
- One open pain point: streaming sometimes cuts out during peak load (partial answers).

Topics:
1) Reliability metrics
2) Streaming behavior under load
3) Next quarter roadmap

Action items:
- Ben to send retry guidance + best practices for SSE clients
- Ava to coordinate support ticket if request_ids confirm a platform issue

Transcript
[00:00] Ben Carter: cool, thanks everyone. wanted to start with last quarters uptime and latency.
[00:38] Jordan Wells: overall good. the one thing that still bites us is streaming. sometimes the response just stops mid-way. no clear error.
[01:05] Ava Chen: to clarify you mean you stop receiving events? like it stalls?
[01:10] Sana Iqbal: yeah. the connection doesnt always close. we just stop getting chunks. our client times out and then we retry. it happens more during our peak traffic window.
[02:02] Dev Patel: is this hosted or dedicated?
[02:05] Jordan Wells: hosted in us-east.
[02:22] Ben Carter: ok. weve been tracking similar reports where SSE gets buffered and it looks like it freezes. we put a keepalive thing in one region; need to confirm rollouts.
[03:10] Ava Chen: can you share a couple request ids? we can pull logs on our side.
[03:18] Sana Iqbal: yes, we can grab a few from our traces.
[40:10] Ben Carter: recap well send retry guidance, and once we have request ids well open an escalation ticket if it looks platform-side.

title: 2026-02-11-palisade-systems-private-onramp-intake-workshop
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-02-11
Start: 08:47 PST
Duration: ~58 minutes
Attendees: Ben Carter (Redwood AE), Rosa Nguyen (Redwood SE), Alex Moreno (CTO, Palisade), Jamie Liu (Network Eng), Priya Shah (Security Ops)

Summary:
Brief intake to understand Palisade's networking constraints for a Private deployment of Redwood. Focus on how they want to connect (VPC peering vs Private Endpoint), region restrictions (EU + US only), egress IPs, KMS integration, and pilot success criteria. Agreed to a technical deep dive next week; action items assigned.

00:00 Ben (AE): ok hey everyone thanks for joining we, uh, appreciate you making time this morning. quick round of intros?
00:08 Alex (CTO): Alex Moreno, CTO at Palisade systems. we own platform infra and want to run inference behind our VPC for PII-sensitive workloads.
00:15 Jamie (Network): Jamie liu, networking, i'll be hands on for peering and firewall rules.
00:19 Priya (Sec): Priya shah, security ops, i'll focus on compliance and key mgmt.
00:23 Ben: great, and i'm Ben, i'll be your AE, Rosa is our solutions engineer. Rosa i'll hand it over to you.
00:30 Rosa (SE): thanks Ben. quick agenda: how you want to connect, cidr/ip constraints, region gating, kms, and pilot scope. we'll try to capture requirements and blockers.
00:45 Alex: high level we have an internal model orchestration platform, and we're evaluating Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private. privacy + logs are the drivers so leaning Private or Dedicated with private networking.
00:56 Jamie: our primary ask is that traffic never traverse the public internet. also need predictable egress IPs for third-party data sources.
01:05 Priya: SOC2 standard controls apply; we need audit logs and bring-your-own-key for encryption at rest. also, data residency: EU workloads must stay in eu-west-1.
01:18 Rosa: makes sense. to clarify, Redwood Private supports two primary connectivity patterns: VPC peering/transit and private endpoints (privatelink style). peering gives lower latency and full VPC access; private endpoints limit surface area but can simplify egress. tradeoffs?
01:37 Jamie: latency matters, but we can't open broad CIDR ranges to other orgs. peering means we have routes, security groups, and we can lock egress to a NAT gateway with static EIP.
01:48 Alex: what about routing scale? we operate across 3 accounts and want to preserve current BGP/transit architecture with a transit gateway. can Redwood peer to a TGW?
01:59 Rosa: yes we support Transit Gateway on AWS and equivalent in other clouds. usually we peer a dedicated account or attach using a VPC peering + route propagation through the TGW. minor note: if you route via TGW there are extra hops and we need to size for bandwidth.
02:15 Ben: also an option is Private Endpoint per model service which terminates TLS at a private IP. reduces lateral exposure but we then need to ensure outbound egress from the service to your VPC for callbacks is handled.
02:29 Priya: callbacks? can you explain?
02:31 Rosa: e.g., model webhooks, function calls, or your backend pulling logs — some operations originate from the Redwood control plane. with private endpoints they're inbound-only to your VPC; if the service needs to call back, we establish an outbound path via a managed NAT in our side or you can route via a peering connection.
02:49 Jamie: hmm interesting. on egress IPs: we need a short list of static IPs to whitelist for our logging and third-party apis. does Redwood provide fixed egress IPs for Private?
02:59 Rosa: for Private deployments we can provide a set of egress IPs per region when you use our managed NAT option; with Dedicated you can also bring your own egress via peering. note sometimes customers use a NAT transit in their VPC and we peer into it so the egress is entirely under your control.
03:17 Alex: region gating is critical. We want inference for eu-west-1 and us-east-1 only. we cannot have any caching or replication in other regions. is that supported?
03:29 Rosa: yes you can pin model versions to specific regions; for Private deployments you deploy Redwood nodes in selected regions only. we won't replicate across regions unless you opt in. we'll also ensure KV cache and checkpoint storage remain in-region.
03:43 Priya: about KMS: we use AWS KMS with external key policies to restrict usage. can Redwood integrate with our KMS or do you require using your key store?
03:55 Rosa: we offer BYOK via customer-managed KMS/HSM; in that case keys stay in your account and we perform envelope encryption. there are some operational steps: cross-account grants and a limited admin role for rotation automation. we'll share the doc.
04:12 Jamie: ok for peering there's a practical question: CIDR overlap. our accounts use 10.0.0.0/16, 10.1.0.0/16 etc. we can't renumber. how does Redwood handle overlapping CIDRs?
04:25 Rosa: overlapping is a common blocker. options: use PrivateLink endpoints which don't require route propagation, or put Redwood in a dedicated account with non-overlapping CIDRs and connect via TGW where we can do NATing/route translation. we'll outline pros/cons and the networking checklist.
04:44 Ben: another consideration: monitoring and logs. Priya you mentioned audit, we can stream audit logs to your S3/CloudWatch in-region. we also support Syslog or an S3 bucket with encryption.
04:59 Priya: need retention policies and proof of deletion for data at rest. can you confirm logs contain no raw customer data?
05:08 Rosa: audit logs are operational — they contain request IDs, timestamps, and metadata. payloads can be redacted. we'll include redaction guarantees in the SOC2 packet we'll share.
05:22 Alex: let's talk throughput and sizing for pilot: initial pilot will be 5k requests/day, spiky bursts up to 400 qps for short periods. how would Dedicated vs Private differ for cost and scalability?
05:36 Rosa: Dedicated gives you reserved pools for predictable throughput, good for sustained burst workloads; Private gives you full control over networking and keying but you manage some scale planning. for pilot we usually start with a small dedicated pool inside your VPC footprint or a hybrid: Hosted API with Private peering for only the pilot's traffic.
05:53 Jamie: what's the rollout pattern? can we canary to only eu-east? (sic) i mean eu-west.

00:45 Alex: high level we have an internal model orchestration platform, and we're evaluating Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private. privacy + logs are the drivers so leaning Private or Dedicated with private networking.
00:56 Jamie: our primary ask is that traffic never traverse the public internet. also need predictable egress IPs for third-party data sources.
01:05 Priya: SOC2 standard controls apply; we need audit logs and bring-your-own-key for encryption at rest. also, data residency: EU workloads must stay in eu-west-1.
01:18 Rosa: makes sense. to clarify, Redwood Private supports two primary connectivity patterns: VPC peering/transit and private endpoints (privatelink style). peering gives lower latency and full VPC access; private endpoints limit surface area but can simplify egress. tradeoffs?

title: POC retention policy matrix & benchmarking kickoff
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-01-29
Start: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~46 minutes
Attendees: Ben Carter (Redwood AE), Maya Singh (Redwood SE), Jordan Kim (Zephyr Head of ML), Priya Rao (Zephyr Security), Marcus Lee (Zephyr Infra)

Summary (auto): kickoff to align on POC success criteria, retention tiers, redaction strategy, and workload/benchmark sizing. Focus on PII classification, prompt storage (ephemeral vs persisted), log telemetry, and throughput/latency SLOs.

Topics (auto): retention policy matrix, on-the-fly redaction vs post-processing, ephemeral prompt vaulting, KV cache implications, benchmark workload distribution, timeline and next steps.

Transcript:
[00:00] Ben (AE): hey everyone, thanks for joining. quick on times — we got about 45 minutes. i'll do a 1 minute intro and hand off to maya for the technical bits.
[00:12] Jordan (Customer): yep thanks — excited to get this scoped. we have a pretty strict retention and audit requirement for pII.
[00:17] Maya (SE): awesome. i'll cover how redwood handles prompt logging, options for ephemeral vs persisted prompt storage, and then we can map that to your retention matrix. plus we'll sketch the benchmark workload.
[00:27] Priya: quick note, from security side we need auditability for 12 months for certain records, and ability to run redaction retroactively. also SSO/SAML and KMS integration are non-negotiable.
[00:39] Ben: understood. on SSO/KMS we have enterprise integrations — i'll attach the security pack — but for the POC we can do a scoped subset.
[00:46] Marcus: from infra, we care about throughput and the tail latency under our expected load. we're targeting p95 < 350 ms for the short-turn conversations and about 100 rps sustained as a pilot.
[00:56] Maya: noted. for the POC benchmarks we'll target two workload profiles: 1) chat-heavy light payloads (mean length ~40 tokens, 70% of traffic), 2) long-context document ops (mean 2k tokens, 30% of traffic). that maps to batching/kv caching behavior.
[01:15] Jordan: and success criteria? apart from latency/cost, we need to measure redaction efficacy — false negative rate for PII detection under our rules.
[01:24] Ben: yeah good call. success criteria proposal: latency p95 < 350ms for chat profile, throughput 100 rps sustained for the pilot VM, cost per 1k tokens under target X (we'll attach numbers), and PII detection FN rate < 1.5% on supplied test set.
[01:38] Priya: 1.5% might be aggressive; we might accept up to 3% if we get strong audit trails and easy deletion. also retention windows differ by data class — personal identifiers (PII) must be deleted or tombstoned after 30 days in the PoC unless otherwise approved, but non-sensitive telemetry can be kept 90 days.
[01:57] Maya: ok, so we'll create a retention policy matrix — column per data class (PII, sensitive, telemetry, meta), rows for storage mode (ephemeral memory, ephemeral encrypted logs, persistent prompt store), then retention default, maximum retention, redaction approach, and exportability.
[02:13] Jordan: can you give an example of 'tombstoning'? is that the same as hashing?
[02:18] Maya: good q — tombstoning in our doc = removing the actual text and keeping a hash/fingerprint so you can still map events but not see content. hashing vs one-way HMAC etc. we support storing one-way fingerprints and you can tie those to your internal IDs in Zephyr.
[02:33] Priya: and how about redaction — on-the-fly redaction before logging vs storing raw and redacting later?
[02:38] Maya: we offer both. on-the-fly redaction means the runtime uses the PII classifier to strip/replace tokens before anything hits logs. down side: you can't re-run a better classifier later. post-processing keeps raw, stores into an encrypted vault, and redaction jobs run later — that gives you re-processability but requires stronger controls (KMS/HSM, RBAC).
[02:58] Marcus: our legal team prefers not storing raw at all. can we do a hybrid — keep ephemeral full payload for N minutes for retries, then tombstone automatically?
[03:07] Ben: yes — we can do ephemeral retention windows. default approach: in-flight KV cache and ephemeral logs held for X minutes (configurable), then automatic purge or tombstone. we'll surface that in the retention matrix.
[03:17] Jordan: what's the effort to integrate Zephyr's PII taxonomy? we have custom fields and a confidence threshold logic.
[03:24] Maya: two options — 1) we hook into your classification API in real-time (we call your classify endpoint via secure VPC peering), or 2) you provide sample rules/regexes we add to the runtime. real-time gives dynamic accuracy but adds latency; the rule-based is faster to deploy.
[03:41] Priya: latency risk is my concern. if classification adds 50-100ms per request that's bad.
[03:47] Maya: right — we can cache classification decisions for common prefixes in the KV cache to avoid repeated calls, but that's only useful for repetitive patterns. for the POC we can measure both modes.
[03:57] Marcus: on the topic of KV cache — does the cache include any raw prompt text? i.e., is there risk of PII lingering in the cache file system?
[04:05] Maya: KEV — sorry KV cache — stores token-level caches. we can configure it to not persist raw prompt text to disk; instead keep only token embeddings/keys and encrypted pointers. for the PoC we can run with memory-only KV and confirm no disk artifacts.
[04:19] Jordan: good. let's talk benchmarks — how will you simulate Zephyr traffic?
[04:24] Ben: we'll use a harness that replays your traffic traces with synthetic anonymization, plus a synthetic generator to hit edge cases. we want 3 runs: baseline hosted config, quantized model config (for cost savings), and dedicated GPU pool to measure tail latencies.
[04:39] Priya: dedicated pool — would that be in Redwood Dedicated or private VPC? we need to see both options for compliance.
[04:45] Maya: for the POC we can provision a temporary Dedicated-like environment in our hosted infra with VPC peering and KMS integration to approximate private. it's not a full private deployment but it'll get us realistic SLOs.
[04:58] Marcus: what about logging levels? we want to separate telemetry vs prompts.
[05:02] Maya: we'll expose logging controls per route: OFF, TELEMETRY_ONLY, PROMPT_HASH_ONLY, FULL_PROMPT_REDAC. you can set per-route retention and redaction rules.
[05:14] Jordan: do you have an example retention matrix we can look at?
[05:18] Ben: yes — maya will send a template. quick run-through now — example rows: PII (names, ssn) -> storage: prompt_hash_only or tombstone after 30d, immediate redaction recommended; Sensitive (medical IDs) -> ephemeral 7d, tombstone; Telemetry -> persistent 90d; Audit logs -> immutable 365d but with access controls.
[05:37] Priya: audit logs being immutable could be a problem if we need deletion requests under certain regs. can audit logs be partitioned and archived off-platform?
[05:44] Maya: yes we can configure archive-and-sanitize flows: move full logs to your S3 with server-side encryption and retention lifecycle policies, and keep only minimal indexes in Redwood for tracing.
[05:55] Jordan: let's pin PII FN target — we'll accept up to 3% FN for PoC as Priya said, with a requirement to run reprocessing if we later push a better classifier. do you support reprocessing from tombstoned hashes?
[06:07] Maya: no — if you tombstone by removing content you'll lose the ability to re-run content-based classifiers. that's why we recommend ephemeral raw retention short window plus robust tombstoning after. but we can store encrypted raw payloads in a customer-managed S3 (you own the key) for reprocessing while Redwood only stores a pointer.
[06:24] Ben: that'll be captured in the matrix as 'pointer-to-encrypted-blob (customer KMS)'.
[06:29] Marcus: timeline — what do we need to hit the benchmarks in 2 weeks?
[06:33] Ben: list: sample traces, PII taxonomy, desired workloads and SLOs, access for Redwood to a staging VPC or at least signed test creds, and we (Redwood) prepare the harness and initial plan.
[06:45] Jordan: we can get traces and taxonomy within 3 business days. staging creds take longer — maybe 7-10 days.
[06:50] Maya: that's fine. our proposed POC schedule: week 0: scoping + data exchange; week 1: infra prep and small smoke benchmarks; week 2: full benchmark runs + redaction efficacy tests; week 3: results, cost estimates, and recommendations.
[07:05] Priya: we need to include a legal checkpoint before any raw data is sent.
[07:09] Ben: agreed — we'll mark that as an action item and not proceed until your legal signs off.
[07:13] Jordan: another Q — how do you surface incidents where prompt content might have leaked in logs?
[07:18] Maya: two mechanisms: 1) tracing and redaction alerts tied to rules; 2) an audit feed that flags any retention policy violations. both can be pushed to your SIEM.
[07:28] Marcus: cost story: if we run quantified models vs full precision how much savings? rough ballpark is fine now.
[07:33] Maya: ballpark: quantization + batching can reduce per-token compute by 2x-3x depending on sequence lengths. we'll include model-specific numbers in the benchmark results.
[07:44] Jordan: ok great. final recap?
[07:46] Ben: recap: Redwood to send retention-policy matrix template and benchmark plan; Zephyr to send traces, taxonomy, and SOC summary; we target initial runs in 2 weeks with a 3-week POC cadence. action items and owners in calendar invite.
[07:58] Priya: i'll add legal to the chain and send an initial redaction-sensitivity doc.
[08:03] Ben: thanks all. we'll follow up with the links and the workplan.
[08:07] Maya: i'll attach the sample matrix and a short doc describing on-the-fly vs post-processing pros/cons.
[08:12] Jordan: thanks everyone. bye.

Notes (auto): transcription contains occasional mis-typed terms (KEV for KV). Several spots had overlapping talk; filler words trimmed for readability. References: security pack link to be shared (redwood.com/security-pack), benchmark harness repo: internal link (redwood-internal/bench-harness).

References / Links noted in call:
- Retention policy matrix template (Redwood) -> will be shared in email
- Benchmark harness repo -> will be shared to Zephyr staging
- Security pack (SOC2/KMS details)

End of transcript.

title: POC weekly: Lo-bit conversion check-in & regression scan
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-10-21
Start: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~56 minutes
Attendees: Alex Kim (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Ben Carter (CSM) - Redwood; Dana Ortiz (ML Lead), Marcus Lee (Infra), Sofia Ruiz (Product), Tom Becker (Data) - Nimbus Health
Links shared: benchmark spreadsheet (drive), regression deck (slides), security FAQ (link)

Summary (auto-gen, may be rough): Quick sync on week-2 lo-bit (int8/fp8) conversion pilot. Reviewed perf deltas, a few regressions on hallucination-sensitive prompts, discussed calibration set and quant params. Agreed next steps to run a longer stability job and share additional failing prompts.

[00:00] Alex: okay hey everyone, thanks for jumping on, we're on for the lo-bit conversion poc weekly, um, objective today is quick results, what changed since last week, any surprises, and next steps. 
[00:28] Dana: great, thanks Alex, hi everyone. we pushed the int8 build into staging last night, ran a few smoke tests, saw throughput improvements but a couple of quality blips on our extraction prompts.
[00:45] Priya: Can you say "extraction prompts" a bit more — are these the structured-medical-record extraction templates?
[00:52] Dana: yeah exactly, like the patient-summary -> medication list parsing. some fields are missing or merged, like "aspirin 81mg" turned into "aspirin81", which breaks downstream parsers.
[01:10] Marcus: from infra we saw p99 token latency drop roughly 22% on average with INT8 with batch size 8, and peak throughput up ~1.8x, but we did notice occasional 500ms spikes at sequence lengths > 1024 when cache warmup happens.
[01:29] Priya: that spike sounds like KV cache population effects. Did you run with prefix caching enabled?
[01:35] Marcus: yes, prefix cache was on, but we had the per-route cache TTL low and some cache thrashing when many sessions hit similar prefixes.
[01:48] Priya: okay makes sense. For the quality issues: did you run symmetric vs asymmetric quantization, and what about group-wise quant?
[01:56] Dana: we used the default conversion script that Alex sent — I think it was symmetric per-tensor? maybe per-channel for some layers, not sure. the sheet says "autofit" which might be Redwood's auto heuristics.
[02:14] Alex: yeah the conversion used our auto-mode: per-channel for weights where applicable, but activation quant was symmetric with a 8-bit clip; it may not be ideal for those NER-like extraction tokens. we also tried an FP8 build in parallel and the FP8 preserved a lot more of the tokenization boundaries, less merging.
[02:36] Sofia: interesting. so FP8 looked better for QA/extraction, but INT8 showed better throughput — is that the quick TL;DR?
[02:44] Priya: short answer yes. FP8 sits in a sweet spot for quality on those sensitive prompts but its perf gains are smaller, depends on kernel support on the hardware. our INF card supports FP8 fast paths for a subset of ops so the delta was about +1.15x on throughput, but INT8 gave +1.8x.
[03:04] Dana: ok, can you quantify the quality drop — what metrics are we looking at? we have accuracy and a custom entity-F1.
[03:11] Priya: we ran the 2.5k prompt validation set: for INT8 we saw entity-F1 drop from 0.92 to 0.86 on average, with a tail of prompts down to 0.75. FP8 was 0.91 average. perplexity doesn't capture the issue here because it's a tokenization boundary error.
[03:35] Tom: we also instrumented the pipeline and saw that the string post-processing regexes were tripped by missing spaces, so that's a brittle point.
[03:43] Ben: is there a quick remediation for those cases? maybe post-quantization token repair heuristics?
[03:50] Priya: a few options: 1) recalibration with a representative calibration set that includes those extraction examples, 2) per-layer higher precision (mixed-bit), 3) FP8 on sensitive routes only, or 4) software fallback for those prompt patterns.
[04:08] Dana: can we do mixed-bit on only the final attention layers?
[04:13] Priya: yes, we can selectively keep some layers in fp16 while quantizing others to int8. that often recovers most of the quality with modest perf loss. we saw in the sandbox that keeping the last two transformer blocks at fp16 recovered entity-F1 to 0.90 while throughput settled at ~1.4x.
[04:33] Marcus: operationally, switching routing to FP8 for a subset of model variants — how heavy is that? can we do that retargeting with a rollout?
[04:40] Alex: yes, Redwood routing can map by policy: e.g., prompt route or customer feature flag to FP8 variant. you can do canary rollouts and automatic fallback to fp16/int8 if latency or error thresholds trigger. I'll attach an example policy.
[04:56] Dana: cool. a question on calibration: how big should the calibration corpus be? we used 2k examples but maybe that's too small or too biased.
[05:05] Priya: recommended is 8k-10k diverse samples for our models for stable clipping statistics, especially for per-channel quant. We'll re-run with the 10k set and include those rare extraction edge-cases.
[05:20] Tom: I can assemble a 10k set from production anonymized records, but need to know format and redaction guidance.
[05:26] Alex: we have a redaction checklist, I'll forward. Ben, can you also share the drive folder link?
[05:31] Ben: yep I'll put the link in the notes. (link pasted)
[05:40] [06:00] Dana: about the hallucination regressions — we noticed when forced decoding of structured fields the model would sometimes invent measurements. Did you see that with FP8?
[06:10] Priya: less with FP8, more with INT8. I think some activation clipping can nudge logits and change the argmax on boundary cases. This shows up as hallucinated numeric values in the extraction outputs.
[06:23] Sofia: is this a function of tokenizer mismatch or quant noise?
[06:27] Priya: mostly quant-induced perturbation. tokenizer mismatch would be more consistent across builds.
[06:33] Dana: ok, we care more about correctness than absolute latency; our SLO is request p95 < 350ms for end-to-end but accuracy is the highest priority.
[06:44] Alex: noted — we can propose a hybrid policy: critical routes map to FP8 or mixed-bit while non-critical analytics routes can use INT8. Also cost math: INT8 reduced token cost by ~38%, FP8 about 22% in our model.
[07:03] Tom: given the cost delta and our usage patterns, maybe a split routing will be fine. we just need a way to automatically shift traffic if quality drops.
[07:10] Priya: Redwood supports automatic rollback on quality regression if you wire up the eval hooks and quality metrics. we can hook in your entity-F1 and set a threshold. If F1 dips below defined value for N minutes we can failover.
[07:25] Marcus: on infra side we also want to know memory footprint differences, because INT8 reduces GPU memory allowing larger batch sizes; FP8 had slightly higher scratch memory due to cast ops.
[07:36] Priya: correct. INT8 reduced peak GPU memory by ~26% enabling batch 12 in our tests, FP8 gave ~12% reduction.
[07:45] Dana: we had one crash during the INT8 run, stacktrace in the sheet points to a kernel fallback in the runtime, something like a non-contiguous tensor path.
[07:56] Priya: hmm that looks like a known edge-case we logged as issue RF-2311 — happens when group-quant size mismatches certain fused ops. we can patch the converter to avoid that path; I'll include the patch in the repro branch.
[08:12] Alex: good, that should be low effort. Dana, can you share the hardware SKU and driver versions? we noted some discrepancies between driver 520.x and 525.x with our kernels.
[08:22] Dana: sure, staging uses ND-accelerator-1 with driver 520.56. Marcus is checking prod driver versions.
[08:29] Marcus: prod is on 525.10, we plan a driver upgrade but want stability first.
[08:33] Priya: driver changes can affect FP8 fast-paths; that might explain a lot of perf variance.
[08:41] Dana: ok. other asks: we want to see more detailed per-layer delta heatmaps, not just aggregate F1.
[08:49] Priya: we have per-layer embeddings drift and token-change matrices; I'll export a heatmap and put in the sheet. you'll see which layers show the biggest KL divergence post-quant.
[08:59] Tom: perfect. also we want to test a stress-run: 48 hour job on canary traffic with 1% real traffic mixed in, to catch stability issues.
[09:08] Marcus: we can spin that up in staging. expect around 150k requests/day at 1% traffic; we'll record errors and latency histograms.
[09:17] Alex: great. timeline wise: Priya re-run calibration by Friday (10/24), Dana provides failing prompt sets by Thursday (10/23), then 48h stability job starts Monday the 10/27 and we reconvene Tuesday the 10/28 for results.
[09:33] Sofia: quick question on monitoring hooks: can we emit entity-level F1 to your observability or do we push metrics to our Prometheus?
[09:40] Ben: both are possible. easiest is push to your Prom and we ingest via remote-write to Redwood console, or we can poll your eval endpoint.
[09:49] Tom: we prefer Prometheus remote-write, we'll open a temporary endpoint.
[09:54] Priya: ok we'll add the hook instructions to the notes.
[10:02] Dana: one last thing — HIPAA implications. any concerns with storing calibration or logs?
[10:08] Alex: Redwood Private is available for HIPAA workloads and we have KMS integrations and audit logs. For now you can use redaction and ephemeral artifacts in staging. we'll also share our security FAQ.
[10:19] Dana: thanks.
[10:22] Alex: quick recap: re-run calibration with 10k, share per-layer heatmaps, assemble failing prompts, start 48h stability run next week, and we'll demo routing/fallback policy. I'll put the policy example and the patch link in the notes.
[10:34] Ben: I'll also attach the benchmark sheet and raw logs.
[10:37] Sofia: thanks team, this was useful.
[10:40] Alex: thanks everyone, talk soon.

Auto topics: quantization, int8, fp8, calibration, KV cache, density-of-errors, throughput, p99 latency, rollout policies, security/HIPAA

Action items (as listed above): Priya to re-run calibration, Dana to supply failing prompts, Alex to send fallback policy, Tom to confirm KV cache flags, start 48h stability run on 2025-10-27.

End of transcript.

title: Field Enablement — Messaging Hypothesis Test
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-09-10 09:00 PT
Duration: 1:18
Attendees: Asha, Miguel, Lena, Ben, Priya, Zahid, Tom, Grace

Summary: Quick internal field enablement to test out refreshed messaging hypotheses on cost/latency tradeoffs, competitive differentiators, and demo playbooks. Run two short roleplays, capture alternative rebuttals, and decide final one-pager updates.

title: Dedicated capacity reservation ops and fallback strategy discussion
preview:
Meeting header:
Date/Time: 2025-09-03 16:00 UTC
Duration: ~46 minutes
Attendees: Alice Johnson (Redwood AE), Ben Carter (Redwood SE), Carlos Reyes (NovusMed CTO), Dana Liu (NovusMed ML Eng), Ellen Park (NovusMed Head of IT)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, rough): NovusMed is evaluating dedicated capacity vs hosted Redwood for inference. Key concerns: data isolation, burst capacity during daily peak, predictable latency for SLAs, and cost. They want a pilot reserved pool, clear failover/fallbacks to hosted or degraded configs, and security proofs (SOC2, KMS integration). Action items and pricing required.

Topics: dedicated reservation sizing, isolation and tenancy (dedicated vs VPC), burst strategy (pre-warm, burst to hosted, queue/backpressure), scaling expectations, benchmark plan, compliance requirements.

Transcript: 
[00:00] Alice Johnson: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. I'm Alice, I'm the AE on the account, and Ben is our SE. Quick check — did I get the time right for everyone?
[00:07] Carlos Reyes: Yep, good here. Thanks Alice. We're excited to get into the nitty gritty of reserved capacity — we ran some numbers and want to see how Redwood handles burst as well as isolation.
[00:15] Ben Carter: Awesome. Quick agenda: we want to understand your peak patterns, what isolation level you need (physical GPU pools vs VPC-only), how you'd like bursts to be handled, and any compliance gating. We'll sketch a pilot. Sound OK?
[00:25] Dana Liu: Sounds good. I'll put some numbers into chat but at a high level: day-to-day we're ~200 concurrent users, model is a 3B transformer-ish open model, and we expect spikes up to 2k qps for short windows after product launches. Latency targets are 200–300ms p50 for generation.
[00:40] Ellen Park: From IT side we care about data not leaking — we need tenant isolation and keys in our KMS. Also audit logs and SSO. SOC2 is a hard check for procurement.
[00:50] Alice Johnson: Great — that's the classic set. On isolation: Redwood Dedicated gives you reserved GPU pools that are isolated from other customers at the scheduler and host level. You can choose single-tenant hardware (physical isolation) or shared hardware with strict tenant scheduling. Which level are you leaning to?
[01:10] Carlos Reyes: We'd prefer physical isolation for ePHI compliance — sorry, that's a bit of a blunt ask — but is that an option for a mid-market team like us?
[01:20] Ben Carter: Yes, physical isolation is available as a Dedicated offering. There is a premium and lead time depending on region. Another option is private deployment (VPC/on-prem) where the control plane can be private and you bring your own keys. That can be on-prem if you need air gap, but it changes support model.
[01:35] Carlos Reyes: Hm, bringing control plane on-prem is heavy. Maybe we can start with physically isolated Dedicated GPUs in a cloud region close to our users. But we need KMS integration so keys never leave NovusMed.
[01:45] Alice Johnson: For KMS we integrate with customer KMS via key wraps — we do not hold plaintext, and HSM integrations are supported for Private deployments. For Dedicated we can integrate with your cloud KMS and also provide audit logs tied to token usage. We'll attach the SOC2 report and KMS integration notes.
[02:05] Dana Liu: About burst: if we reserve a baseline of, say, 8 GPUs for steady traffic how does Redwood handle a sudden 6x spike? Do you autoscale? Is there bursting to shared capacity?
[02:20] Ben Carter: Good question. There are a few levers: 1) Reserved pool baseline with autoscaling of additional dedicated nodes within the customer's quota; 2) Burst to a pre-provisioned burst pool (reserved but smaller, cheaper to keep warm); 3) Fallback routing to Redwood Hosted (not physically isolated) in emergencies; 4) Graceful degradation like lower batch size or forceful early-exit tokens to reduce latency. Each has pros/cons on isolation and cost.
[02:45] Carlos Reyes: Hmm. The fallback to hosted sounds risky for ePHI, won't pass compliance. Would only be acceptable if we can strip PII or route non-sensitive traffic.
[02:55] Ben Carter: Totally understood — many customers split traffic. You can annotate requests with data classification and have routing rules: sensitive -> Dedicated only, non-sensitive -> Hosted fallback allowed. Redwood Console supports route-level policies.
[03:10] Ellen Park: And the audit trails for that split — we need full logging to show any switch over.
[03:15] Ben Carter: Yes, auditing is in the Console — per-token traces, model version, node id, and timestamped fallbacks.
[03:25] Dana Liu: On autoscaling speed — how fast can you spin an extra GPU for a burst? Our spikes are short, like 5–10 minutes. If spin-up is 10+ minutes it's not useful.
[03:35] Ben Carter: Startup times depend on the instance type and region; for warm reserved pools we recommend maintaining a small hot reserve that can take load within 60–90s. Cold spin-up from scratch can be several minutes. For very short spikes, we recommend a hybrid: keep minimal hot reserve plus small burst pool that is billed as reserved but at lower capacity to reduce cost.
[03:55] Carlos Reyes: So roughly the recommendation is baseline reserved for steady qps, a warm reserve for quick spikes, and then a larger cold reserve if needed for longer events?
[04:05] Ben Carter: Yes. Also consider prefix/KV cache—if many requests share prefixes the effective throughput improves. That was part of Redwood Optimize we can run against your prompt set.
[04:15] Alice Johnson: Quick commercial note — reserved pools have options: monthly flex or annual commit. Annual gives better pricing. There's also a burst pricing add-on that controls how often you can burst above baseline without renegotiating capacity. We'll include pricing options.

title: Northwind Analytics x Redwood - POC Check-in
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-08-01
Time: 11:00 AM PT
Duration: 38 min
Attendees: Ben Carter (Redwood), Aisha Rahman (Redwood), Samir Qureshi (Northwind), Nina Gomez (Northwind)

Summary
Northwind reports intermittent timeouts when using Redwood hosted API structured output with JSON Schema. Redwood shares mitigations and confirms an in-progress config change in us-east.

Transcript
[00:00] Ben Carter: Hey Samir, Nina. Thanks for jumping on. Hows the POC going overall?
[00:14] Samir Qureshi: Pretty good, except were kind of stuck on the structured output thing. When we enable the JSON schema, well hit timeouts. Like not always, but enough to break the workflow.
[00:33] Nina Gomez: Yeah its the same prompt. If we turn schema off, it returns. With schema on, sometimes it just hangs and then we hit 60 seconds on our side.
[00:49] Aisha Rahman: Got it. That matches what were seeing on our side: long tail latency on the constrained decoding path. Do you remember if its worse with the bigger schema?
[01:03] Samir Qureshi: Yeah, bigger schema is definitely worse. Ours is like, dozens of fields, nested arrays, and we had a few any-of sections.
[01:18] Ben Carter: Okay. Two things to try immediately: reduce max output tokens, and simplify the schema. The anyOf/oneOf can blow up the constraint space.
[01:34] Samir Qureshi: That makes sense. But we need the schema ideally. Do you have a recommended limit?
[01:45] Aisha Rahman: We dont have a hard limit published yet. Internally were adding more guardrails. For now well share best practices: keep it flat, avoid combinators, and if you need optional branches, consider tool calling.
[02:09] Nina Gomez: Tool calling worked but our downstream system expects JSON in a very specific format, thats why the schema thing is nice.
[02:23] Ben Carter: Totally. Also were rolling out a mitigation in us-east for enterprise traffic that extends the internal step timeout. It should reduce the gateway timeouts youre seeing.
[02:38] Samir Qureshi: Is that already live?
[02:42] Aisha Rahman: Not yet, but soon. Well email when its fully deployed.
[03:02] Samir Qureshi: Okay. Just to confirm: this isnt only us, right?
[03:07] Ben Carter: No, weve had at least one other report. Were treating it as a known issue and well publish a doc so your team has something to reference.
[03:23] Nina Gomez: Cool. If you can share that doc plus guidance, we can adjust the schema and keep going.
[03:35] Ben Carter: Sounds good. Lets align on next steps: well send schema best practices + confirm mitigation timing; you test with a simplified schema and possibly tool-calling fallback for the larger ones.
[03:53] Samir Qureshi: Yep.

title: Post-launch retro - Benchmark report campaign (KPI review + refresh plan)
preview: Team reviewed performance of the public benchmark report launch (landing page + interactive table + PDF + blog + webinar). Results were strong top-of-funnel with meaningful sales-assisted pipeline influence, but launch-day reliability issues (cache miss storm + 429s) created support load and some negative chatter. The methodology page and reproducibility artifacts were consistently cited as the trust driver. Next phase focuses on (1) tightening follow-up loops for high-intent leads, (2) shipping a lightweight “replicate this in your PoC” guide and a clearer metric explainer, (3) improving cache/CDN settings and guardrails for future spikes, and (4) establishing a refresh cadence with clear material-change thresholds and legal review gates.

title: Benchmark report launch webinar rehearsal (run of show + demo + Q&A)
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-07-08

Duration: 58 min
Title: Benchmark report launch webinar rehearsal (run of show + demo + Q&A)

Attendees
- Noura El-Khoury (producer)
- Soojin Lee (PMM / host)
- Ethan Wallace (content)
- Jonas Weber (Applied ML / performance)
- Claire Dubois (Applied ML / methodology)
- Alicia Perez (design)
- Felix Schneider (web engineering)
- Ben Carter (growth)
- Wei Chen (docs / copy)
- Markus Klein (solutions engineering)

---

Summary (auto)
- Run through the webinar agenda and tighten speaker transitions.
- Validate the live demo flow for the interactive benchmark page.
- Practice answering common “fairness” and “can you replicate this?” questions.
- Capture last-minute edits for slides, copy, and disclaimers.

Questions (auto)
- Are we saying “latency per dollar” or “latency / $” out loud?
- How do we explain caching/batching without sounding like we tuned for the benchmark?
- What do we do if the landing page is slow or 429s?
- How do we avoid competitor callouts but still contextualize?

---

[00:00] Noura El-Khoury: Alright, cool. Recording is on. This is our rehearsal for next week’s benchmark report launch webinar. Goal today: timing, speaker handoffs, demo run-through, and we’ll do a Q and A bank at the end.

[00:15] Soojin Lee: Yep. And reminder, this is the public one, so anything we say should match the final copy and legal-safe language. No “best in the world”, none of that.

[00:24] Ethan Wallace: I wrote that on a sticky note. No absolutes.

[00:28] Noura El-Khoury: Great. I’ll be keeping time and I’ll interrupt if we drift. We’re aiming for 30 minutes of content, 10 minutes live demo, 15 Q&A, plus buffer.

[00:39] Noura El-Khoury: Soojin, you’re the host. You kick. Ethan does the narrative setup. Jonas and Claire split “what we measured.” Alicia + Felix cover demo. Then Soojin closes with CTA and Ben’s follow-up. Sound right?

[00:52] Soojin Lee: Yep.

[00:54] Noura El-Khoury: Okay, let’s do a dry run. Soojin, go.

[01:00] Soojin Lee: Hi everyone, thanks for joining. I’m Soojin, product marketing at Redwood Inference. Today we’re sharing our new benchmark report focused on production inference economics: latency and cost efficiency, or what we call latency per dollar—

[01:13] Wei Chen: Quick interruption, we decided to say “latency per dollar” aloud and show “latency/$” in the UI, right?

[01:18] Soojin Lee: Yes. Thanks. Latency per dollar. We’ll show the metric as latency slash dollar in the table.

[01:25] Soojin Lee: —and why we think measuring just tokens per second isn’t enough for production teams. We’ll cover how we measured, what we found across models and regions, and how you can use the interactive table and download the PDF.

[01:39] Noura El-Khoury: Good. Keep that to about 90 seconds in the real thing.

[01:43] Soojin Lee: Got it. Then I’ll hand to Ethan.

[01:46] Soojin Lee: Ethan, over to you.

[01:48] Ethan Wallace: Cool. So the “why” here: when teams ship LLM features, performance isn’t just raw speed. It’s speed under load, consistency across regions, and the cost you pay to hit your SLOs. We built this report because people ask, “What model is fastest?” and that’s not the right question. The right question is: for my workload, in my region, what’s the best tradeoff between latency, reliability, and cost.

[02:14] Jonas Weber: Can we say “best” there?

[02:16] Ethan Wallace: Right, sorry. “What’s the right tradeoff” between latency and cost.

[02:21] Soojin Lee: That’s the pattern: “in our tests,” “for the workloads measured,” “depending on constraints.”

[02:27] Ethan Wallace: Yep. In our tests, across a curated set of models and regions, we saw meaningful differences in cost efficiency that don’t show up if you only look at throughput.

[02:39] Noura El-Khoury: Nice. Ethan, you’re at ~50 seconds. In the live, you have 4 minutes total, so you can include the “production economics” framing and the quick caveat about variance.

[02:49] Ethan Wallace: Got it.

[02:51] Noura El-Khoury: Next, Jonas + Claire: methodology section. Keep it crisp and don’t go too deep on internals.

[02:58] Jonas Weber: Okay. I’ll do the “what is latency per dollar” and Claire does “how we ran it.”

[03:03] Claire Dubois: Sure.

[03:05] Jonas Weber: Latency per dollar is our normalized metric that combines the latency distribution—p50 and p95—and the cost to generate tokens for a defined workload. The idea is not that one number replaces everything; it’s a way to compare cost efficiency while keeping latency honest.

[03:24] Markus Klein: We should say “cost per 1M tokens” too, because prospects ask for that.

[03:28] Jonas Weber: Yes. We also provide cost per one million output tokens and throughput, and we always show p50 and p95 latency.

[03:37] Soojin Lee: Minor: “output tokens” specifically, so no confusion.

[03:41] Jonas Weber: Right, cost per 1M output tokens.

[03:44] Claire Dubois: On methodology: we used our prompt set v3, designed to reflect a production mix—some prefill-heavy prompts, some decode-heavy. We ran at defined concurrency levels, did warmups, and we applied stability gates. If p95 latency variance exceeded a threshold, we reran automatically.

[04:06] Felix Schneider: Claire, should you mention caching? It’s one of the top questions.

[04:10] Claire Dubois: Yes. We used Redwood’s default production serving settings for each deployment mode tested. We did not create special one-off tuning for the benchmark. Some optimizations like continuous batching are part of standard serving; caching policy is described in the methodology section.

[04:26] Wei Chen: Careful: “default” can be interpreted. Maybe “standard recommended configuration for this workload class” or “documented configuration.”

[04:33] Claire Dubois: Good point. We used documented, reproducible configurations, and we publish the configuration details and caveats.

[04:42] Noura El-Khoury: Great. Jonas + Claire, in the real webinar you have 6 minutes combined. Keep those four bullets: prompt mix, concurrency + warmups, stability rerun gates, and where to find the full methodology.

[04:55] Soojin Lee: Then I come back for a quick “what you’ll see in the table” bridge, right?

[04:59] Noura El-Khoury: Yep. Do that and then hand to demo.

[05:03] Soojin Lee: Okay: “Now we’ll show the interactive page where you can filter by model and region, compare p95 latency, and export data.”

[05:11] Soojin Lee: Alicia, Felix, take it away.

[05:14] Alicia Perez: Cool. So on screen, we have the landing page. Above the fold is the key chart and below is the interactive table. I’ll scroll quickly and highlight the three main controls: model filter, region filter, and context length / workload preset.

[05:29] Felix Schneider: I’ll drive the demo live. Just flagging: the current build has the “latency/$” label with a little info icon, but the tooltip copy is still draft.

[05:38] Noura El-Khoury: We’ll capture that as an action item.

[05:41] Felix Schneider: Demo flow: first, show “All models, NA region.” Then click to EU West. Show that p95 changes. Then switch to a longer context bucket and point out “results vary by workload.” Then click “Export CSV” and show it downloads.
