title: Hightouch intro - reverse ETL for PLG signals to HubSpot
preview:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Meeting: Hightouch intro - reverse ETL for PLG signals to HubSpot
Date/Time: May 9, 2025 10:05 AM PT
Duration: 44 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees
- Ben Carter (Redwood Inference, Growth)
- Logan Wright (Redwood Inference, Telemetry & Analytics)
- David Kim (Redwood Inference, HubSpot Admin / Biz Systems)
- Tejas Manohar (Hightouch, CEO)
- Shreya Patel (Hightouch, Solutions Engineer)
- Alex Chen (Hightouch, Partnerships)

Summary (auto-generated)
Redwood described a new “PLG-to-sales signal routing” project to detect product usage thresholds and intent signals and route them into HubSpot with ownership and task creation. Hightouch discussed reverse ETL patterns, identity resolution options, and governance controls for pushing warehouse-derived signal tables into HubSpot without exposing unnecessary PII. The group compared build vs buy for enrichment + routing, talked about HubSpot API limits, idempotency/deduping, and security considerations (SOC 2, scopes, retention, access controls).

Topics
1) Current Redwood pipeline + where reverse ETL could fit
2) Signal table modeling (daily/weekly windows), scoring, dedupe
3) HubSpot objects: company properties vs custom object + timeline notes
4) Identity resolution (workspace/account mapping)
5) Security + privacy: PII minimization, retention, SOC2, audit logs
6) Operational concerns: retries, HubSpot 429s, batching

Questions (captured)
- Should signals live as a HubSpot custom object (“PLG Signal”) or flattened onto the Company record with a timeline note?
- What’s the cleanest key strategy: HubSpot company_id vs domain vs internal account_id?
- Can we enforce suppression windows (don’t create tasks if marketing nurture already touched within X days) in Hightouch workflows?
- How do we keep “false positives” from creating repeated tasks (idempotency + re-notification thresholds)?

Transcript

[00:00] Ben Carter: Cool. Thanks everyone for hopping on. I’m Ben, I run growth here at Redwood. We’re kicking off a project to route product-led signals into HubSpot, and we’ve been hearing “reverse ETL could help” so we wanted to do an intro with Hightouch.

[00:16] Alex Chen: Great to meet you all. I’m Alex, partnerships at Hightouch. Tejas is on, and Shreya from solutions engineering, she’ll go deeper on architecture.

[00:25] Tejas Manohar: Hey folks. Thanks for having us.

[00:28] Shreya Patel: Hi everyone.

[00:31] Ben Carter: Quick context: We’re PLG-led, self-serve API. As folks scale, we want to detect signals like sustained token volume, spend velocity, repeated 429s, security doc views, Dedicated/Private docs interest, and team expansion, and then create HubSpot tasks with ownership rules. The big constraints: minimize spam, privacy-aware, and avoid breaking HubSpot with rate limits.

[01:01] Logan Wright: I’ll add: the raw telemetry is big. We already aggregate daily into an analytics dataset. We have a scoring service that produces a “qualified signal” output, but enrichment and identity resolution is messy.

[01:18] David Kim: And on the HubSpot side, we’ve got custom properties and workflows drafted. We’ve also seen HubSpot 429s when we burst create tasks and timeline events.

[01:31] Shreya Patel: Okay, makes sense. The classic reverse ETL fit is: you have a warehouse table that represents the “business state” — in your case, a daily signal table per account — and then you sync that into HubSpot on a schedule with upsert semantics.

[01:47] Ben Carter: When you say “upsert,” you mean like match on company_id or domain?

[01:51] Shreya Patel: Exactly. Typically either HubSpot object ID, or a stable key like domain. Many teams do domain, but for B2B SaaS you often have edge cases: multiple domains, subsidiaries, free email, etc.

[02:08] Logan Wright: Yeah. We have workspace_id internally. Some workspaces are personal trials with gmail. Some are legit companies but early.

[02:17] Shreya Patel: Then we’d recommend a mapping table: workspace_id -> account_id -> preferred_domain (if known) -> hubspot_company_id (once resolved). The sync can be staged: first get company_id, then push updates.

[02:33] David Kim: For HubSpot, company_id is best for accuracy. But we don’t always have it at signal time. Also: you can’t always search by domain reliably because HubSpot allows multiple companies with same domain if someone created duplicates.

[02:49] Tejas Manohar: One thing we see is folks using Hightouch not only for syncing but for “identity stitching” — pulling from different sources, de-duping, and then making sure the CRM gets a clean record.

[03:03] Ben Carter: That’s helpful. Our question is: do we keep the routing service we’re building (we have a thin service that calls HubSpot APIs directly) or do we offload part to Hightouch.

[03:15] Shreya Patel: There are a few patterns:
- Pattern A: You keep your router service, and Hightouch is only enrichment (e.g., add firmographics, region, employee count) back into your warehouse or into HubSpot.
- Pattern B: You generate final “tasks to create” rows in the warehouse and Hightouch executes the sync into HubSpot.
- Pattern C: Hybrid — keep task creation in your app (because tasks are a bit tricky), but use Hightouch to keep company properties and lists up to date.

[03:47] David Kim: Tasks are the tricky part. HubSpot tasks aren’t as clean for upsert, right?

[03:52] Shreya Patel: Yeah, tasks are more “create event” than “current state.” Reverse ETL is best at maintaining current state fields. For tasks, we can still do it, but you need idempotency keys and a writeback strategy.

[04:08] Logan Wright: We already have dedupe keys per signal: like account_id + signal_type + window_start. That can be used as an idempotency key.

[04:18] Shreya Patel: Perfect. If you store the “last_synced_at” and “hubspot_task_id” as a writeback column, then next run you can decide to skip.

[04:29] Ben Carter: But then we’re storing HubSpot IDs in the warehouse.

[04:32] Shreya Patel: That’s pretty common. Alternatively you can store it in your app DB if you prefer. But the point is you need some place that persists “we already created the task.”

[04:45] David Kim: Also rate limits. We had a Jira around 429s. If we do it through Hightouch, does that help?

[04:53] Tejas Manohar: We have built-in retry/backoff, concurrency controls, and you can configure batch sizes. So yes, it tends to smooth out bursts. But HubSpot’s rate limits still exist.

[05:08] Ben Carter: Our current service does exponential backoff too, but it still can pile up if we’re trying to create tasks + timeline notes for a bunch of companies at once.

[05:19] Shreya Patel: For that, you usually want queueing. Hightouch effectively acts as a managed queue for sync operations.

[05:29] Logan Wright: Can Hightouch also enforce suppression? Like, don’t create a task if there was a task in the last 14 days, or if marketing just sent a lifecycle email.

[05:40] Shreya Patel: Yes, if the suppression logic is representable in SQL (or dbt) and materialized into the table. For example you’d compute “should_route = true/false” with a suppression window, or compute “next_eligible_at.”

[05:58] Ben Carter: That’s good because one of our biggest risks is spam and false positives. Sales team will revolt.

[06:05] David Kim: Marketing also. We’re trying not to double-touch.

[06:10] Alex Chen: We see teams build a “touch calendar” table — last outbound touch by channel — and then use that in routing conditions.

[06:20] Logan Wright: We have product events and some HubSpot activity data. Pulling HubSpot activity back into the warehouse is possible but…

[06:28] Shreya Patel: That’s actually a really common loop. We call it “writeback” or “reverse + forward” sync. You can ingest HubSpot engagements into the warehouse, then your routing logic is aware of current outreach.

[06:44] David Kim: Is it incremental? HubSpot activity is huge.

[06:48] Shreya Patel: Usually incremental by updated_at. But you should scope: you may only need engagement counts and last-touch timestamps by company.

[06:59] Ben Carter: Let’s talk about security and privacy. We’re trying to be careful about what we push to HubSpot. We want a “privacy-aware” signal object. What do you typically recommend?

[07:12] Tejas Manohar: Principle of least privilege. Only sync what sales needs to act: signal type, severity/score, and context like “spend up 3x” without raw prompts or user-level PII.

[07:27] Logan Wright: Right, we’re not sending prompt content. The telemetry includes request metadata but we can aggregate to account-level metrics.

[07:35] David Kim: HubSpot is a CRM; it’s not a telemetry store.

[07:40] Shreya Patel: Exactly. We’d recommend: aggregated metrics (e.g., 7-day token volume bucket, 30-day error rate bucket), a score, and maybe a “top reason” field. Avoid sending IPs, emails unless necessary.

[07:56] Ben Carter: What about doc views? That can tie to an email.

[08:01] Logan Wright: Our docs instrumentation sees user_id. We can roll up “security page viewed by at least one user in workspace in last 7 days.”

[08:10] Shreya Patel: That’s the right approach: boolean intent flags at the account level.

[08:16] David Kim: And compliance: SOC 2, data residency. Hightouch’s posture?

[08:23] Alex Chen: We can share our SOC 2 report process and security overview. We do have SOC 2 Type II. For data residency, depends on the deployment, but generally we can run in US regions; EU is something to discuss depending on your constraints.

[08:41] Shreya Patel: Also, regarding access controls: we integrate to your warehouse using a service account with read access to specific schemas/tables. You can scope it to a dedicated “signals” schema.

[08:55] Logan Wright: That would make our security folks happier. Today our router service reads from analytics tables, but it also has access to a bunch of raw stuff.

[09:05] Ben Carter: We have an internal privacy review thread that’s basically “minimize fields to HubSpot” and “define retention.” If Hightouch is in the middle, does that expand the scope?

[09:17] Tejas Manohar: It adds a processor, yes. But in exchange you can reduce custom code and tighten data contracts. Many regulated customers do this with vendor due diligence.

[09:30] David Kim: Also OAuth scopes to HubSpot. Today our app has fairly broad scopes.

[09:36] Shreya Patel: Hightouch uses OAuth or private app tokens depending on your preference. You can scope to only objects you need: companies, custom objects, engagements/tasks if you go that route.

[09:51] Ben Carter: We also need auditability: “why was this task created” and “who triggered it.”

[09:57] Logan Wright: We have a dedupe key and a signal version.

[10:01] Shreya Patel: Great. Then in HubSpot you can store the dedupe key and a “signal_version” property. For audit logs, you can keep the source rows in the warehouse plus Hightouch run logs.

[10:16] David Kim: HubSpot timeline notes are helpful for reps. But those are just text blobs.

[10:21] Ben Carter: We currently plan: set company properties for “latest signal score,” “latest signal type,” then append a timeline note with details. And create a task.

[10:34] Shreya Patel: That’s a good UX. The only warning: timeline notes can get spammy too. You might want to only write notes when the signal is “qualified” or crosses a tier.

[10:46] Logan Wright: That’s our plan. We have threshold + intent combos, plus suppression windows.

[10:53] Ben Carter: Let’s get concrete. Say: repeated 429s + spend growth. That’s an “upgrade to Dedicated” signal. In our table we’d have account_id, signal_type, score, window_start, context fields. Hightouch syncs to HubSpot company.

[11:10] Shreya Patel: Yup. And you can map region: if account is EU, route to EMEA owner. That can be a property used by HubSpot workflow.

[11:21] David Kim: Ownership rules in HubSpot are… messy. We have “preserve existing owner if set,” else round robin by region, and special cases for existing customers with a CSM.

[11:34] Shreya Patel: Usually we don’t set owner directly from reverse ETL unless it’s deterministic. Instead: sync the routing inputs (region, tier, existing_csm flag), and let HubSpot workflows assign owner.

[11:51] Ben Carter: That aligns with our plan. We want deterministic logic, but executed in HubSpot workflows.

[11:58] Logan Wright: Where Hightouch helps is getting those routing inputs correct and consistent.

[12:04] Shreya Patel: Exactly.

[12:07] Ben Carter: What about enrichment? We talked to our partnerships folks about Clearbit-type enrichment but we’re cautious.

[12:15] Alex Chen: We integrate with a few enrichment providers, but you can also bring your own. The main question is whether enrichment data is allowed in your privacy policy and whether you want it in HubSpot.

[12:29] Logan Wright: For now we might just use what we already have: billing country, cloud region, and maybe inferred company domain.

[12:37] Tejas Manohar: Start simple. Reverse ETL can be valuable even without third-party enrichment.

[12:44] David Kim: Another technical detail: HubSpot custom objects. We considered a “PLG Signal” custom object for history. But sales reps may not click into it.

[12:56] Shreya Patel: We see two approaches:
1) Keep only “latest signal” fields on Company + timeline notes for history.
2) Create a custom object for full history and show it in the right sidebar.

[13:10] Ben Carter: And the pro of custom object is structured reporting.

[13:14] David Kim: Yes, but the con is more complexity.

[13:17] Shreya Patel: You can also do a hybrid: custom object for reporting, and an abbreviated timeline note for rep readability.

[13:28] Logan Wright: If we do custom object, can Hightouch upsert it reliably?

[13:33] Shreya Patel: Yes, HubSpot custom objects support upserts if you have a unique property. You’d set unique dedupe key as the external ID.

[13:44] David Kim: That might be nice. We can avoid duplicating signal entries.

[13:48] Ben Carter: We’re worried about “re-notification”: if a signal persists across weeks, we don’t want 10 objects. We want maybe one object updated, plus only create a new task when it crosses a threshold.

[14:03] Shreya Patel: That’s a very common pattern. You keep the signal object as “current state,” and have another table that triggers “notify events.” Notify events can be suppressed and idempotent.

[14:17] Logan Wright: Our scoring service can output both: “stateful signal” and “notification event.”

[14:21] Ben Carter: Exactly.

[14:24] David Kim: From HubSpot side, tasks are basically the notify events.

[14:29] Shreya Patel: Then I’d recommend: use Hightouch for the stateful sync (company properties, lists, maybe custom signal object), and keep your in-house service or a lightweight mechanism for tasks if you want more control.

[14:44] Ben Carter: That sounds like Pattern C you mentioned.

[14:47] Shreya Patel: Yeah.

[14:49] Tejas Manohar: It’s also a risk reduction approach. You don’t have to replatform everything at once.

[14:56] Ben Carter: Let’s talk about data retention. We’re drafting a policy: signal data in the warehouse has X retention; in HubSpot we keep only what’s needed for sales follow-up. How does Hightouch store data?

[15:11] Shreya Patel: Hightouch is primarily a conduit. We run sync jobs; we store job logs and metadata. We don’t need to persist your underlying tables long-term. Any caching is short-lived.

[15:24] David Kim: And logs — can we restrict who sees the logs?

[15:27] Alex Chen: Yes, RBAC within Hightouch. We can share more on roles/permissions.

[15:33] Logan Wright: Another question: can Hightouch run inside our VPC? Probably not in this phase, but…

[15:40] Alex Chen: There are deployment options depending on plan, but for most customers it’s SaaS. If you have strict VPC requirements, we can discuss.

[15:52] Ben Carter: For this project, we’re not doing full Private deployment for internal tooling. But we need to pass security review.

[16:01] Tejas Manohar: Understood.

[16:03] (crosstalk)

[16:05] David Kim: Sorry, one more HubSpot-specific thing: workflows and lists. If Hightouch sets a property, HubSpot workflows can trigger task creation. That might be the cleanest.

[16:17] Shreya Patel: Exactly. You can sync a property like “plg_signal_qualified = true” plus “plg_signal_type = scale_429” plus “plg_signal_score = 82”. Workflow triggers, assigns owner, creates task, sends internal notification.

[16:35] Ben Carter: And then we reset plg_signal_qualified back to false?

[16:38] David Kim: Or we could store a timestamp “last_qualified_at” and use workflow re-enrollment rules.

[16:45] Shreya Patel: Timestamp is usually better than boolean.

[16:49] Logan Wright: We can compute “last_qualified_at” per account per signal type.

[16:53] Ben Carter: Then suppression: only update if last_qualified_at changes beyond suppression window.

[17:01] Shreya Patel: Yup.

[17:04] Ben Carter: Can Hightouch help with “round robin”? Or should that stay in HubSpot.

[17:10] Shreya Patel: Round robin should stay in HubSpot workflows. Hightouch isn’t a CRM assignment engine.

[17:16] David Kim: Good.

[17:18] Ben Carter: Okay. Next: what does implementation look like? Timeline wise we’re piloting NA first.

[17:26] Alex Chen: Usually initial setup is: connect warehouse, define model/table, map fields to HubSpot, test with a small cohort, then expand.

[17:36] Shreya Patel: For your case, we’d want to see your proposed signal table schema and keys. Then we can recommend the most stable upsert keys and a writeback plan.

[17:48] Logan Wright: Our warehouse is Snowflake.

[17:50] Shreya Patel: Great, Snowflake is common.

[17:53] Ben Carter: And we have a “signals_scored_daily” table (name not final). Each row per account per day per signal type.

[18:02] Shreya Patel: Then you might also have “signals_notifications” for task triggers.

[18:06] Ben Carter: Exactly.

[18:09] David Kim: On HubSpot properties: we need to define which properties are single-value vs multi. HubSpot doesn’t have arrays. So we’ll probably store top signal type and top score.

[18:23] Logan Wright: We can compute “primary_signal_type” and “secondary_signal_type” maybe.

[18:28] Ben Carter: But don’t overdo it.

[18:31] Shreya Patel: Agree. Also: store a URL back to your internal dashboard or analytics view for the signal details. Reps can click out.

[18:41] David Kim: That’s a good idea. We can generate a link like /signals/account/<id>?window=30d.

[18:48] Logan Wright: As long as it’s access controlled.

[18:51] Ben Carter: Yep.

[18:53] Tejas Manohar: Another thought: many teams use reverse ETL to keep lifecycle stages accurate (e.g., “PQL” or “PQA”). If you want a product-qualified lead stage based on signals, it’s a natural fit.

[19:10] Ben Carter: We do want a PQL stage but we’re being careful because false positives create bad handoffs.

[19:17] Logan Wright: We’re introducing confidence thresholds.

[19:20] Shreya Patel: You can incorporate confidence and only flip stage when confidence > X for Y days.

[19:29] David Kim: But stage changes trigger lots of things.

[19:32] Ben Carter: For pilot, we might keep it as tasks only, not stage changes.

[19:36] Shreya Patel: That’s reasonable.

[19:39] Ben Carter: Any gotchas we should be aware of?

[19:42] Shreya Patel: A few:
- HubSpot API limits; be careful with high-cardinality objects.
- Dedupe keys: be explicit, otherwise you’ll create duplicates.
- Identity matching: domain-only can be dangerous. Prefer HubSpot company_id when available.
- Suppression: enforce in the source table so you don’t have to “undo” things.

[20:09] David Kim: What about deleting data? If a customer requests deletion, does Hightouch complicate it?

[20:16] Shreya Patel: If the source of truth is your warehouse and you delete from there, the next sync can propagate deletions depending on configuration. For HubSpot, you can also clear properties. Engagements/tasks are harder to delete automatically; you’d want a policy.

[20:35] Ben Carter: Our policy is probably: don’t store user PII in HubSpot from signals, so deletion requests are less likely to impact. But we still need to be able to remove account-level signal metadata if asked.

[20:49] David Kim: We can clear those properties.

[20:52] Shreya Patel: Exactly.

[20:55] Logan Wright: Another operational issue: backfills. Sometimes we rerun aggregation for the last 7 days.

[21:02] Shreya Patel: Backfills are okay, as long as upsert keys are stable. You’ll want to avoid “late arriving” rows causing re-triggers. That’s where notify tables should include “created_at” and “already_notified” flags.

[21:20] Ben Carter: Makes sense.

[21:22] (brief audio glitch)

[21:26] Ben Carter: Sorry, cut out. Okay. I think we’re aligned that reverse ETL is a fit mainly for syncing state into HubSpot and possibly supporting suppression via pulling engagement data back.

[21:40] Tejas Manohar: Yep.

[21:42] Alex Chen: And we can follow up with a sample architecture doc.

[21:46] David Kim: Can you include OAuth scope recommendations? We’re trying to keep it minimal.

[21:52] Alex Chen: Yes.

[21:54] Ben Carter: Great. Next steps: we’ll send you the signal schema and a sample payload we’re currently writing to HubSpot (company props, timeline note text). We’ll also include our constraints around privacy.

[22:09] Logan Wright: I can send details on aggregation windows and event lag assumptions. Daily job, with about 2-4 hour lag depending on telemetry.

[22:18] Shreya Patel: Perfect.

[22:20] David Kim: I’ll send a list of HubSpot objects we plan to touch: Companies, maybe custom object “PLG Signal,” and Tasks. Plus the rate limit notes.

[22:33] Ben Carter: And then we schedule a short technical follow-up with our security reviewer. Probably Marcus Lin or Aisha Rahman, depending.

[22:42] Alex Chen: Works.

[22:44] Shreya Patel: One more thing — you mentioned enrichment. If you decide you want firmographics later, we can do it, but it’s optional.

[22:52] Ben Carter: For now, optional.

[22:55] Tejas Manohar: Sounds good.

[22:57] Ben Carter: Alright. Thanks everyone. Appreciate the time.

[23:01] Alex Chen: Thanks all.

[23:03] Shreya Patel: Thank you.

[23:04] Tejas Manohar: Thanks.

(Recording ends shortly after; remaining time is mostly silence / tool cutoff.)

As folks scale, we want to detect signals like sustained token volume, spend velocity, repeated 429s, security doc views, Dedicated/Private docs interest, and team expansion, and then create HubSpot tasks with ownership rules. The big constraints: minimize spam, privacy-aware, and avoid breaking HubSpot with rate limits.

[01:01] Logan Wright: I’ll add: the raw telemetry is big. We already aggregate daily into an analytics dataset. We have a scoring service that produces a “qualified signal” output, but enrichment and identity resolution is messy.

[01:18] David Kim: And on the HubSpot side, we’ve got custom properties and workflows drafted. We’ve also seen HubSpot 429s when we burst create tasks and timeline events.

[01:31] Shreya Patel: Okay, makes sense. The classic reverse ETL fit is: you have a warehouse table that represents the “business state” — in your case, a daily signal table per account — and then you sync that into HubSpot on a schedule with upsert semantics.

[01:47] Ben Carter: When you say “upsert,” you mean like match on company_id or domain?

[01:51] Shreya Patel: Exactly. Typically either HubSpot object ID, or a stable key like domain. Many teams do domain, but for B2B SaaS you often have edge cases: multiple domains, subsidiaries, free email, etc.

[02:08] Logan Wright: Yeah. We have workspace_id internally. Some workspaces are personal trials with gmail. Some are legit companies but early.

[02:17] Shreya Patel: Then we’d recommend a mapping table: workspace_id -> account_id -> preferred_domain (if known) -> hubspot_company_id (once resolved). The sync can be staged: first get company_id, then push updates.

[02:33] David Kim: For HubSpot, company_id is best for accuracy. But we don’t always have it at signal time. Also: you can’t always search by domain reliably because HubSpot allows multiple companies with same domain if someone created duplicates.

[02:49] Tejas Manohar: One thing we see is folks using Hightouch not only for syncing but for “identity stitching” — pulling from different sources, de-duping, and then making sure the CRM gets a clean record.

[03:03] Ben Carter: That’s helpful. Our question is: do we keep the routing service we’re building (we have a thin service that calls HubSpot APIs directly) or do we offload part to Hightouch.

[03:15] Shreya Patel: There are a few patterns:
- Pattern A: You keep your router service, and Hightouch is only enrichment (e.g., add firmographics, region, employee count) back into your warehouse or into HubSpot.
- Pattern B: You generate final “tasks to create” rows in the warehouse and Hightouch executes the sync into HubSpot.
- Pattern C: Hybrid — keep task creation in your app (because tasks are a bit tricky), but use Hightouch to keep company properties and lists up to date.

[03:47] David Kim: Tasks are the tricky part. HubSpot tasks aren’t as clean for upsert, right?

[03:52] Shreya Patel: Yeah, tasks are more “create event” than “current state.” Reverse ETL is best at maintaining current state fields. For tasks, we can still do it, but you need idempotency keys and a writeback strategy.

[04:08] Logan Wright: We already have dedupe keys per signal: like account_id + signal_type + window_start. That can be used as an idempotency key.

[04:18] Shreya Patel: Perfect. If you store the “last_synced_at” and “hubspot_task_id” as a writeback column, then next run you can decide to skip.

[04:29] Ben Carter: But then we’re storing HubSpot IDs in the warehouse.

[04:32] Shreya Patel: That’s pretty common. Alternatively you can store it in your app DB if you prefer. But the point is you need some place that persists “we already created the task.”

[04:45] David Kim: Also rate limits. We had a Jira around 429s. If we do it through Hightouch, does that help?

[04:53] Tejas Manohar: We have built-in retry/backoff, concurrency controls, and you can configure batch sizes. So yes, it tends to smooth out bursts. But HubSpot’s rate limits still exist.

[05:08] Ben Carter: Our current service does exponential backoff too, but it still can pile up if we’re trying to create tasks + timeline notes for a bunch of companies at once.

[05:19] Shreya Patel: For that, you usually want queueing. Hightouch effectively acts as a managed queue for sync operations.

[05:29] Logan Wright: Can Hightouch also enforce suppression? Like, don’t create a task if there was a task in the last 14 days, or if marketing just sent a lifecycle email.

[05:40] Shreya Patel: Yes, if the suppression logic is representable in SQL (or dbt) and materialized into the table. For example you’d compute “should_route = true/false” with a suppression window, or compute “next_eligible_at.”

[05:58] Ben Carter: That’s good because one of our biggest risks is spam and false positives. Sales team will revolt.

[06:05] David Kim: Marketing also. We’re trying not to double-touch.

[06:10] Alex Chen: We see teams build a “touch calendar” table — last outbound touch by channel — and then use that in routing conditions.

[06:20] Logan Wright: We have product events and some HubSpot activity data. Pulling HubSpot activity back into the warehouse is possible but…

[06:28] Shreya Patel: That’s actually a really common loop. We call it “writeback” or “reverse + forward” sync. You can ingest HubSpot engagements into the warehouse, then your routing logic is aware of current outreach.

[06:44] David Kim: Is it incremental? HubSpot activity is huge.

[06:48] Shreya Patel: Usually incremental by updated_at. But you should scope: you may only need engagement counts and last-touch timestamps by company.

[06:59] Ben Carter: Let’s talk about security and privacy. We’re trying to be careful about what we push to HubSpot. We want a “privacy-aware” signal object. What do you typically recommend?

[07:12] Tejas Manohar: Principle of least privilege. Only sync what sales needs to act: signal type, severity/score, and context like “spend up 3x” without raw prompts or user-level PII.

[07:27] Logan Wright: Right, we’re not sending prompt content. The telemetry includes request metadata but we can aggregate to account-level metrics.

[07:35] David Kim: HubSpot is a CRM; it’s not a telemetry store.

[07:40] Shreya Patel: Exactly. We’d recommend: aggregated metrics (e.g., 7-day token volume bucket, 30-day error rate bucket), a score, and maybe a “top reason” field. Avoid sending IPs, emails unless necessary.

[07:56] Ben Carter: What about doc views? That can tie to an email.

[08:01] Logan Wright: Our docs instrumentation sees user_id. We can roll up “security page viewed by at least one user in workspace in last 7 days.”

[08:10] Shreya Patel: That’s the right approach: boolean intent flags at the account level.

[08:16] David Kim: And compliance: SOC 2, data residency. Hightouch’s posture?

[08:23] Alex Chen: We can share our SOC 2 report process and security overview. We do have SOC 2 Type II. For data residency, depends on the deployment, but generally we can run in US regions; EU is something to discuss depending on your constraints.

[08:41] Shreya Patel: Also, regarding access controls: we integrate to your warehouse using a service account with read access to specific schemas/tables. You can scope it to a dedicated “signals” schema.

[08:55] Logan Wright: That would make our security folks happier. Today our router service reads from analytics tables, but it also has access to a bunch of raw stuff.

[09:05] Ben Carter: We have an internal privacy review thread that’s basically “minimize fields to HubSpot” and “define retention.” If Hightouch is in the middle, does that expand the scope?

[09:17] Tejas Manohar: It adds a processor, yes. But in exchange you can reduce custom code and tighten data contracts. Many regulated customers do this with vendor due diligence.

[09:30] David Kim: Also OAuth scopes to HubSpot. Today our app has fairly broad scopes.

[09:36] Shreya Patel: Hightouch uses OAuth or private app tokens depending on your preference. You can scope to only objects you need: companies, custom objects, engagements/tasks if you go that route.

[09:51] Ben Carter: We also need auditability: “why was this task created” and “who triggered it.”

[09:57] Logan Wright: We have a dedupe key and a signal version.

[10:01] Shreya Patel: Great. Then in HubSpot you can store the dedupe key and a “signal_version” property. For audit logs, you can keep the source rows in the warehouse plus Hightouch run logs.

[10:16] David Kim: HubSpot timeline notes are helpful for reps. But those are just text blobs.

[10:21] Ben Carter: We currently plan: set company properties for “latest signal score,” “latest signal type,” then append a timeline note with details. And create a task.

[10:34] Shreya Patel: That’s a good UX. The only warning: timeline notes can get spammy too. You might want to only write notes when the signal is “qualified” or crosses a tier.

[10:46] Logan Wright: That’s our plan. We have threshold + intent combos, plus suppression windows.

[10:53] Ben Carter: Let’s get concrete. Say: repeated 429s + spend growth. That’s an “upgrade to Dedicated” signal. In our table we’d have account_id, signal_type, score, window_start, context fields. Hightouch syncs to HubSpot company.

[11:10] Shreya Patel: Yup. And you can map region: if account is EU, route to EMEA owner. That can be a property used by HubSpot workflow.

[11:21] David Kim: Ownership rules in HubSpot are… messy. We have “preserve existing owner if set,” else round robin by region, and special cases for existing customers with a CSM.

[11:34] Shreya Patel: Usually we don’t set owner directly from reverse ETL unless it’s deterministic. Instead: sync the routing inputs (region, tier, existing_csm flag), and let HubSpot workflows assign owner.

[11:51] Ben Carter: That aligns with our plan. We want deterministic logic, but executed in HubSpot workflows.

[11:58] Logan Wright: Where Hightouch helps is getting those routing inputs correct and consistent.

[12:04] Shreya Patel: Exactly.

[12:07] Ben Carter: What about enrichment? We talked to our partnerships folks about Clearbit-type enrichment but we’re cautious.

[12:15] Alex Chen: We integrate with a few enrichment providers, but you can also bring your own. The main question is whether enrichment data is allowed in your privacy policy and whether you want it in HubSpot.

[12:29] Logan Wright: For now we might just use what we already have: billing country, cloud region, and maybe inferred company domain.

[12:37] Tejas Manohar: Start simple. Reverse ETL can be valuable even without third-party enrichment.

[12:44] David Kim: Another technical detail: HubSpot custom objects. We considered a “PLG Signal” custom object for history. But sales reps may not click into it.

[12:56] Shreya Patel: We see two approaches:
1) Keep only “latest signal” fields on Company + timeline notes for history.
2) Create a custom object for full history and show it in the right sidebar.

[13:10] Ben Carter: And the pro of custom object is structured reporting.

[13:14] David Kim: Yes, but the con is more complexity.

[13:17] Shreya Patel: You can also do a hybrid: custom object for reporting, and an abbreviated timeline note for rep readability.

[13:28] Logan Wright: If we do custom object, can Hightouch upsert it reliably?

[13:33] Shreya Patel: Yes, HubSpot custom objects support upserts if you have a unique property. You’d set unique dedupe key as the external ID.

[13:44] David Kim: That might be nice. We can avoid duplicating signal entries.

[13:48] Ben Carter: We’re worried about “re-notification”: if a signal persists across weeks, we don’t want 10 objects. We want maybe one object updated, plus only create a new task when it crosses a threshold.

[14:03] Shreya Patel: That’s a very common pattern. You keep the signal object as “current state,” and have another table that triggers “notify events.” Notify events can be suppressed and idempotent.

[14:17] Logan Wright: Our scoring service can output both: “stateful signal” and “notification event.”

[14:21] Ben Carter: Exactly.

[14:24] David Kim: From HubSpot side, tasks are basically the notify events.

[14:29] Shreya Patel: Then I’d recommend: use Hightouch for the stateful sync (company properties, lists, maybe custom signal object), and keep your in-house service or a lightweight mechanism for tasks if you want more control.

[14:44] Ben Carter: That sounds like Pattern C you mentioned.

[14:47] Shreya Patel: Yeah.

[14:49] Tejas Manohar: It’s also a risk reduction approach. You don’t have to replatform everything at once.

[14:56] Ben Carter: Let’s talk about data retention. We’re drafting a policy: signal data in the warehouse has X retention; in HubSpot we keep only what’s needed for sales follow-up. How does Hightouch store data?

[15:11] Shreya Patel: Hightouch is primarily a conduit. We run sync jobs; we store job logs and metadata. We don’t need to persist your underlying tables long-term. Any caching is short-lived.

[15:24] David Kim: And logs — can we restrict who sees the logs?

[15:27] Alex Chen: Yes, RBAC within Hightouch. We can share more on roles/permissions.

[15:33] Logan Wright: Another question: can Hightouch run inside our VPC? Probably not in this phase, but…

[15:40] Alex Chen: There are deployment options depending on plan, but for most customers it’s SaaS. If you have strict VPC requirements, we can discuss.

[15:52] Ben Carter: For this project, we’re not doing full Private deployment for internal tooling. But we need to pass security review.

[16:01] Tejas Manohar: Understood.

[16:03] (crosstalk)

[16:05] David Kim: Sorry, one more HubSpot-specific thing: workflows and lists. If Hightouch sets a property, HubSpot workflows can trigger task creation. That might be the cleanest.

[16:17] Shreya Patel: Exactly. You can sync a property like “plg_signal_qualified = true” plus “plg_signal_type = scale_429” plus “plg_signal_score = 82”. Workflow triggers, assigns owner, creates task, sends internal notification.

[16:35] Ben Carter: And then we reset plg_signal_qualified back to false?

[16:38] David Kim: Or we could store a timestamp “last_qualified_at” and use workflow re-enrollment rules.

[16:45] Shreya Patel: Timestamp is usually better than boolean.

[16:49] Logan Wright: We can compute “last_qualified_at” per account per signal type.

[16:53] Ben Carter: Then suppression: only update if last_qualified_at changes beyond suppression window.

[17:01] Shreya Patel: Yup.

[17:04] Ben Carter: Can Hightouch help with “round robin”? Or should that stay in HubSpot.

[17:10] Shreya Patel: Round robin should stay in HubSpot workflows. Hightouch isn’t a CRM assignment engine.

[17:16] David Kim: Good.

[17:18] Ben Carter: Okay. Next: what does implementation look like? Timeline wise we’re piloting NA first.

[17:26] Alex Chen: Usually initial setup is: connect warehouse, define model/table, map fields to HubSpot, test with a small cohort, then expand.

[17:36] Shreya Patel: For your case, we’d want to see your proposed signal table schema and keys. Then we can recommend the most stable upsert keys and a writeback plan.

[17:48] Logan Wright: Our warehouse is Snowflake.

[17:50] Shreya Patel: Great, Snowflake is common.

[17:53] Ben Carter: And we have a “signals_scored_daily” table (name not final). Each row per account per day per signal type.

[18:02] Shreya Patel: Then you might also have “signals_notifications” for task triggers.

[18:06] Ben Carter: Exactly.

[18:09] David Kim: On HubSpot properties: we need to define which properties are single-value vs multi. HubSpot doesn’t have arrays. So we’ll probably store top signal type and top score.

[18:23] Logan Wright: We can compute “primary_signal_type” and “secondary_signal_type” maybe.

[18:28] Ben Carter: But don’t overdo it.

[18:31] Shreya Patel: Agree. Also: store a URL back to your internal dashboard or analytics view for the signal details. Reps can click out.

[18:41] David Kim: That’s a good idea. We can generate a link like /signals/account/<id>?window=30d.

[18:48] Logan Wright: As long as it’s access controlled.

[18:51] Ben Carter: Yep.

[18:53] Tejas Manohar: Another thought: many teams use reverse ETL to keep lifecycle stages accurate (e.g., “PQL” or “PQA”). If you want a product-qualified lead stage based on signals, it’s a natural fit.

[19:10] Ben Carter: We do want a PQL stage but we’re being careful because false positives create bad handoffs.

[19:17] Logan Wright: We’re introducing confidence thresholds.

[19:20] Shreya Patel: You can incorporate confidence and only flip stage when confidence > X for Y days.

[19:29] David Kim: But stage changes trigger lots of things.

[19:32] Ben Carter: For pilot, we might keep it as tasks only, not stage changes.

[19:36] Shreya Patel: That’s reasonable.

[19:39] Ben Carter: Any gotchas we should be aware of?

[19:42] Shreya Patel: A few:
- HubSpot API limits; be careful with high-cardinality objects.
- Dedupe keys: be explicit, otherwise you’ll create duplicates.
- Identity matching: domain-only can be dangerous. Prefer HubSpot company_id when available.
- Suppression: enforce in the source table so you don’t have to “undo” things.

[20:09] David Kim: What about deleting data? If a customer requests deletion, does Hightouch complicate it?

[20:16] Shreya Patel: If the source of truth is your warehouse and you delete from there, the next sync can propagate deletions depending on configuration. For HubSpot, you can also clear properties. Engagements/tasks are harder to delete automatically; you’d want a policy.

[20:35] Ben Carter: Our policy is probably: don’t store user PII in HubSpot from signals, so deletion requests are less likely to impact. But we still need to be able to remove account-level signal metadata if asked.

[20:49] David Kim: We can clear those properties.

[20:52] Shreya Patel: Exactly.

[20:55] Logan Wright: Another operational issue: backfills. Sometimes we rerun aggregation for the last 7 days.

[21:02] Shreya Patel: Backfills are okay, as long as upsert keys are stable. You’ll want to avoid “late arriving” rows causing re-triggers. That’s where notify tables should include “created_at” and “already_notified” flags.

[21:20] Ben Carter: Makes sense.

[21:22] (brief audio glitch)

[21:26] Ben Carter: Sorry, cut out. Okay. I think we’re aligned that reverse ETL is a fit mainly for syncing state into HubSpot and possibly supporting suppression via pulling engagement data back.

[21:40] Tejas Manohar: Yep.

[21:42] Alex Chen: And we can follow up with a sample architecture doc.

[21:46] David Kim: Can you include OAuth scope recommendations? We’re trying to keep it minimal.

[21:52] Alex Chen: Yes.

[21:54] Ben Carter: Great. Next steps: we’ll send you the signal schema and a sample payload we’re currently writing to HubSpot (company props, timeline note text). We’ll also include our constraints around privacy.

[22:09] Logan Wright: I can send details on aggregation windows and event lag assumptions. Daily job, with about 2-4 hour lag depending on telemetry.

[22:18] Shreya Patel: Perfect.

[22:20] David Kim: I’ll send a list of HubSpot objects we plan to touch: Companies, maybe custom object “PLG Signal,” and Tasks. Plus the rate limit notes.

[22:33] Ben Carter: And then we schedule a short technical follow-up with our security reviewer. Probably Marcus Lin or Aisha Rahman, depending.

[22:42] Alex Chen: Works.

[22:44] Shreya Patel: One more thing — you mentioned enrichment. If you decide you want firmographics later, we can do it, but it’s optional.

[22:52] Ben Carter: For now, optional.

[22:55] Tejas Manohar: Sounds good.

[22:57] Ben Carter: Alright. Thanks everyone. Appreciate the time.

[23:01] Alex Chen: Thanks all.

[23:03] Shreya Patel: Thank you.

[23:04] Tejas Manohar: Thanks.

(Recording ends shortly after; remaining time is mostly silence / tool cutoff.)

title: ABM pilot kickoff - regulated verticals (finserv + healthcare)
preview: Internal kickoff for the 6–8 week vertical ABM pilot targeting regulated workloads. Team aligned on primary goals (validate repeatable positioning + outbound motion, ship durable vertical assets, generate qualified pipeline for Dedicated/Private). Discussed whether to start with financial services only vs running finserv + healthcare in parallel; decision: prioritize finserv for weeks 1–4, keep healthcare assets in draft and pull forward if bandwidth allows / if we get warm signals. Reviewed asset plan (landing page, one-pagers, security FAQ + evidence pack index, outbound sequences, deck/battlecard, webinar outline, metrics dashboard), named owners, and set weekly cadence. Flagged key risks: compliance-safe claims (avoid “HIPAA-ready”), security questionnaire turnaround time, limited vertical proof points/logos, and inconsistent packaging guidance between Dedicated vs Private. Closed with concrete next steps and review gates for external copy.

title: BluePeak AI - quickstart + first production launch checklist demo
preview: Redwood demoed the in-Console onboarding + “first production launch” checklist prototype and walked BluePeak AI through the updated Hosted API quickstart (streaming chat, retries/timeouts, and observability basics). BluePeak’s main concerns were around quota/rate-limit surprises, cost controls (hard budget caps vs alerts), and how quickly usage dashboards reflect spend. Redwood clarified quota vs rate limits, pointed to budget alert options and recommended client-side backoff patterns, and proposed next steps: enable a sandbox tenant for BluePeak, share the checklist doc links and a production readiness quickstart, and schedule a follow-up focused on quotas/budgets and rollout/fallback policies.

title: Lattice Search — security + production readiness review (Hosted API + roadmap)
preview: Lattice Search walked Redwood through their internal go-live/security approval requirements for adding an LLM inference provider to an enterprise search product (customer data, regulated customers). Key asks: auditability (who did what / when), SSO/SAML + RBAC, data retention and residency clarity, and a concrete “launch checklist” they can attach to their change-management packet. Redwood clarified Hosted API data handling (no prompt storage by default; limited logs), available security controls today (SOC 2, encryption, key mgmt options depending on deployment), and what’s on the near-term roadmap (richer audit logs in Console, admin controls). Discussion also covered production readiness items: retries/timeouts, rate limiting/quotas, streaming disconnect behavior, and model pinning/fallback. Agreed to share Redwood’s security artifacts + draft production launch checklist, and schedule a technical deep dive on audit logs and Private/VPC options.

title: Security review - Redwood Inference with Northbridge Health
preview:
Meeting Header
Date: 2024-11-12
Time: 2:00pm ET
Duration: 41 min
Attendees: Ben Carter (Redwood), Priya Natarajan (Redwood), Samantha Yuen (Northbridge Health), Miguel Ortega (Northbridge Health), Rina Shah (Northbridge Health)

Summary (auto)
- Discussed audit logging, SOC 2, and data retention options.
- Customer asked about reducing retention for troubleshooting artifacts.

Transcript
[00:00] Ben Carter: Cool, thanks everyone for joining. Quick overview, Redwood does hosted inference and we also have private deployments.
[00:32] Samantha Yuen: Our main questions are around PHI. Do you store prompts or responses?
[01:05] Priya Natarajan: We don't use customer data to train. For hosted, we have limited retention for debugging and abuse prevention, and access is controlled.
[02:10] Miguel Ortega: Right. Were not necessarily asking for zero, but we want the minimum. Whats the shortest retention you can do? Like 7 days?
[03:02] Ben Carter: Hosted has a fixed window today. We can share the exact number offline. In private deployments, you have more control over logging and retention.
